content:
Shipping software has never been easier. Understanding it still is.

5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few months ago, I built a small internal tool over a weekend. What would have taken me several evenings in the past took only a few hours with AI. I described features, copied code, refined prompts, and watched entire modules appear almost instantly.

• At first, it felt magical. 🚀

• Authentication? Done.

• Database models? Done.

• API endpoints? Done.

• Frontend components? Done.
I remember leaning back in my chair thinking:
“Wow. This changes everything.”
Then production happened. A seemingly harmless feature started causing random failures. API calls were timing out. Database queries slowed down dramatically. Logs became increasingly noisy. Fixing one bug introduced two more.
What should have been a 20-minute debugging session turned into an entire weekend of tracing dependencies, reviewing generated code, and questioning my life choices.
That’s when I realized something important:
Vibe coding is incredibly fast. Debugging it isn’t.
And I suspect many developers are beginning to discover the same thing.
The New Developer Divide
AI has dramatically lowered the barrier to writing code. Today, almost anyone can generate a working application with tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or GitHub Copilot. Features that once required days can now be built in hours.
But I’ve noticed a growing gap emerging:

• People who can generate code.

• People who actually understand systems.
The difference becomes painfully obvious when something breaks. Generating code is easy. Explaining why the system suddenly makes 50 unnecessary API calls under load is not.
Generating a database schema is easy. Understanding why that schema causes performance bottlenecks six months later is much harder. AI can often produce code that looks elegant on the surface while hiding architectural problems underneath.
And those problems rarely appear on day one. They appear at 2:13 AM on a Sunday.
Code That Works Isn’t Necessarily Good Code
One of the biggest misconceptions I see is the assumption that working code equals good engineering.
It doesn’t.
I’ve reviewed AI-generated code that was perfectly functional but suffered from issues like:

• Tight coupling between components.

• Excessive database queries.

• Poor separation of concerns.

• Duplicate business logic.

• Hidden security vulnerabilities.

• Unnecessary complexity.

• The code passed tests.

• The demo worked.

• Everyone was happy.

• Until maintenance began.
Software engineering has always been about trade-offs. Every architectural decision affects scalability, maintainability, performance, security, and future development speed. AI can suggest implementations. But understanding those trade-offs still requires human judgment.
My Most Expensive AI Mistake
One project taught me this lesson the hard way. I asked an AI assistant to generate several backend endpoints for a dashboard application.
The generated code looked fantastic.

• Clean functions.

• Helpful comments.

• Proper error handling.

• I merged it quickly.
A week later, users reported that dashboard pages felt sluggish. After profiling the application, I discovered the problem. The generated implementation was making dozens of separate database queries for every request.
For a handful of users, this wasn’t noticeable. Under real traffic, performance collapsed. The AI had optimized for correctness, not efficiency.
Fixing the issue required redesigning the data access layer entirely. Ironically, rewriting the code manually took less time than debugging the original implementation.
Lesson learned:
Readable code is not always scalable code.
AI Doesn’t Remove Technical Debt
Technical debt isn’t new. Developers have been creating questionable code since the invention of programming.
The difference is speed. Previously, writing poor code still required effort. Today, AI allows teams to generate enormous amounts of code almost instantly. This means organizations can accumulate technical debt at unprecedented speed.
I’ve seen developers respond to bugs by simply asking AI to generate additional fixes.

• Sometimes those fixes work.

• Sometimes they introduce entirely new layers of complexity.
It’s a bit like repairing a leaking pipe by continuously wrapping it with more duct tape. Eventually, you stop seeing the pipe. You only see tape. And eventually, everything explodes. 💥🤣
Why Fundamentals Matter
Ironically, AI hasn’t reduced the importance of fundamentals. It’s increased their value. When everyone can generate code, the scarce skill becomes understanding. The developers who stand out are increasingly the ones who can:
Design systems
System design remains difficult because it involves trade-offs rather than syntax.
Questions like:

• Should this service be synchronous or asynchronous?

• Should we denormalize this database?

• Where should caching occur?

• How do we handle failures gracefully?
These decisions determine whether software survives production.
Debug effectively
Debugging is often detective work. Logs, metrics, traces, stack traces, infrastructure behavior, and business logic all intersect.
AI can assist.
But someone still needs to ask the right questions.
Review code critically
I increasingly believe that modern programming is becoming less about writing code and more about reviewing it.
Developers now need to evaluate:

• Is this implementation maintainable?

• Is it secure?

• Will it scale?

• Is there a simpler solution?

• What assumptions does this code make?
The ability to smell “something feels wrong here” is becoming incredibly valuable.
Understand infrastructure
Applications don’t exist in isolation. They run on servers, containers, databases, networks, and cloud platforms. When production incidents occur, infrastructure knowledge often matters more than framework knowledge.
Junior Developers Shouldn’t Panic
Whenever I discuss AI and software engineering, someone inevitably asks:
“What should beginners focus on now?”
My answer is always the same. Learn the fundamentals. Seriously.
Learn:

• Data structures and algorithms.

• Databases.

• Networking basics.

• Operating system concepts.

• System design.

• Debugging techniques.

• Testing strategies.

• Security fundamentals.
And most importantly:
Write code yourself.
Reading AI-generated code can create the illusion of understanding. Writing software from scratch exposes every gap in your knowledge. You discover what you truly understand.
And what you don’t.
Those lessons are incredibly valuable.
The Future Belongs to Engineers
I use AI every single day. It has unquestionably made me more productive. I would not voluntarily go back. But I’ve stopped viewing AI as a replacement for engineering expertise. Instead, I see it as an amplifier.

• Strong engineers become dramatically faster.

• Weak foundations become dramatically more visible.
The developers who thrive over the next decade won’t necessarily be the people who generate the most code. They’ll be the people who understand systems deeply enough to know when the generated code is brilliant — and when it’s complete nonsense.
Because shipping software is only half the job.
Keeping it alive is the hard part. 😄
